title: Sticky gingerbread loaf
subtitle: Dark, treacly gingerbread that improves as it keeps
excerpt: A deeply flavoured, sticky gingerbread loaf made with black treacle and dark muscovado sugar. It comes out of the oven dense and slightly damp, and becomes stickier and more intense over the following two days.
sourceNotes: Gingerbread in Britain traces to medieval spiced breads sweetened with honey and later treacle. The sticky loaf version (as distinct from dry gingernuts or firm gingerbread men) is particularly associated with Yorkshire, where parkin (made with oatmeal and syrup) and sticky gingerbread are regional staples. Elizabeth David's English Bread and Yeast Cookery (1977), Jane Grigson's English Food (1974), and Delia Smith's Winter Collection (1995) all document versions of this dark, treacly loaf.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool and wrap in foil. Freeze for up to 3 months. Thaw slowly at room temperature, still wrapped, overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The gingerbread is better after 24 to 48 hours. Wrap in foil and keep at room temperature. It will become progressively stickier and more flavourful for up to 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; cover with foil after 35 minutes if top colours too fast

content.text: This gingerbread does not look ready when it is ready. The centre will appear slightly sunken and the surface will feel tacky. That is correct. It firms and stickifies as it cools, and the flavour deepens over the following days. Resist cutting it until it is at least completely cold.

Measure the black treacle and golden syrup by weight, not by spoon. Warm the tin briefly in hot water first: the syrups slide out cleanly and weigh accurately. Cold treacle clings to every surface and is difficult to remove completely.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin. Put the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{muscovado-dark}}, {{black-treacle}}, and {{golden-syrup}} in a saucepan over a low heat. Stir until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{whole-milk}}. Leave to cool for 5 minutes, then beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time.

Sift the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{mixed-spice}} into a large bowl. Pour in the warm treacle mixture and stir until smooth and pourable. The batter will be very liquid. Pour into the tin and bake for 45 to 50 minutes. The loaf is done when a skewer inserted in the deepest part of the centre comes out clean and the surface has set firm to the touch (the top may still appear slightly damp but should not wobble). Cool in the tin for 30 minutes before turning out. Wrap in foil and leave overnight before cutting.
